Uma criança que quase não fala, que evita contato visual, que emite sons fora de hora ou que faz movimentos estranhos o tempo todo. Tanto que na década de 50, na primeira edição do Manual Diagnóstico dos Transtornos Mentais, o DSM, o autismo foi classificado como um tipo de esquizofrenia. Aquela condição em que as pessoas ouvem vozes e ficam com pensamento fora da realidade. Mas é claro que a ciência foi avançando até entender na década de 80 e 90 que o autismo é um transtorno do neurodesenvolvimento. A ciência descobriu que por conta de alterações cerebrais que acontecem ainda na gestação, o autista se comporta e se comunica de forma diferente, fora do socialmente esperado. e que na verdade aquele perfil de pessoa estranha, retraída e que quase não fala era só um dos muitos perfis que as pessoas autistas podem apresentar. A Passos lentos, foi só em 2013 que foi criado o termo transtorno do espectro autista para englobar todas as subcategorias. Hoje a ciência entende que cada autista vai ter uma combinação diferente de características e comportamentos divididas em dois grandes blocos. Um, dificuldades persistentes de comunicação e interação social. e dois, padrões restritos e repetitivos de comportamentos, interesses ou atividades. white, black, yellow.  In today's video , I'm going to reveal the mystery of what the skin color of the first human being was, based on the most recent studies about our origins.  Has the enormous diversity of skin colors that we see today always existed?  Or could it all have started with one color that gave rise to all the others?  Was the first Homo sapiens that appeared in Africa black like the people who live there today, or was it white?  To fully understand the evolution of our most striking characteristic, I need to tell you what biologically defines our skin color.  Look at this map that shows the distribution of skin colors of human beings in each region of the world. Can you see a pattern?  Darker skin tones tend to be concentrated in the region with the highest incidence of sunlight, near the equator.  And this relationship between sun and skin color is no coincidence, because the molecule that gives skin its pigmentation, melanin, also acts as a natural sunscreen. It absorbs ultraviolet light from the sun, preventing DNA damage and keeping the cell from burning easily.  Melanin is produced by cells called melanocytes, which are scattered throughout the eyes, scalp, and, of course, the skin.  After producing melanin, melanocytes package this pigment into tiny microscopic capsules and distribute this material to surrounding cells.  It's as if the melanocytes were paint factories, and the other cells gradually received this paint .  With the exception of albinos, who do not produce melanin well due to genetic reasons, or those who have a disease that destroys melanocytes, such as vitiligo, we all produce melanin. But the difference between a lighter- skinned European from northern Finland, a darker-skinned South African, an Asian, and an indigenous person lies in the type and amount of melanin that the melanocytes produce. There are two main types of melanin in the body.  Eumelanin, which has a dark brown or black hue.  It is more efficient at absorbing solar radiation.  And the higher the proportion of melanin, the darker the skin, eyes, and hair tend to be. Pheomelanin, on the other hand, has more yellowish or reddish tones.  She is more associated with features such as red hair, freckles, and also greenish eye color.  Now, in cases where melanocytes produce very little of both types of melanin, the low pigmentation results in very fair skin, blond hair, or blue eyes.  So, have you been able to identify what type of melanin predominates in your body?  Comment below.  Interestingly, the genes that control melanin production in melanocytes work independently in the skin, hair, and eyes.  That's why a person with dark skin and a lot of pheomelanin can have light eyes with more pheomelanin, just as people with lighter skin can have dark eyes and black hair.  Melanin in one area does not influence melanin in another.  It is the ratio between the quantity and types of melanin that produces an enormous diversity of skin tones, one of the most striking characteristics of human beings, so much so that it becomes a source of pride or, unfortunately, prejudice. At least that's what we see today.  But have we always been this different?  What color was the first human?  The answer came when scientists looked at where Homo sapiens originated.  The oldest fossils of our species, Homo sapiens, were found on the African continent, dating back 200,000 to 300,000 years.   It's still unclear exactly where humanity originated, the place where we came from.  Some very old fossils were discovered in what is now Ethiopia and Kenya, and for a long time it was believed that people came from there. But other, older fossils were found in Morocco.  So we don't know exactly, but regardless of the exact country, the fact is that we emerged and dominated the African continent.  But living in Africa 300,000 years ago wasn't exactly easy.  The sun was intense, the savanna was open, it was hot all day long, and survival depended on one thing: running.  Running for hours chasing prey or fleeing from a predator.  And the most widely accepted hypothesis about the bodily changes our ancestors underwent to reach us, which allowed them to survive in that environment, was the loss of body hair.  Excessive hair prevents the dissipation of body heat, which can overheat the body and even cause death.  With less body hair, Homo sapiens was able to sweat and dissipate heat much more efficiently.  And generation after generation, the ancestors with less hair survived better and passed this trait on.  It's OK?  But what is the relationship between hair loss and skin color? So, if you shave the fur off a chimpanzee, what do you find? Lightly pigmented skin, almost pink.  It is likely that our hominid ancestors, before Homo sapiens, such as australopithecines and Homer erectus, who still had hair, had lighter skin .  But when this hair disappeared in the case of Homo sapiens, the light skin would be directly exposed to the African sun, which is a practically impossible condition and incompatible with life.  Extreme UV radiation can cause burns and damage to the DNA of skin cells, and it also has an even more serious effect that often goes unnoticed. UV radiation degrades folic acid, an essential nutrient for embryo formation and blood cell production. Without sufficient folic acid, humans would develop severe anemia, and women would be unable to bear healthy children.  In practice, with our light-colored skin exposed, our species would have become extinct before it even became established.   That is why , generation after generation, over hundreds of thousands of years, those with darker skin were selected, survived longer, and had more children.  So, when Homo sapiens had already emerged as a species, black skin was already the norm; it was simply the only viable option for living in that region.  But if the first humans were black, how did all the diversity we see today come about?  What explains white skin and different skin tones? Now, if all Homo sapiens humans originated with black skin, let's find out how lighter skin came about. Until recently, the answer to that question seemed to follow a pattern. Humans left Africa, migrated to colder regions with less sun, and their skin lightened to facilitate the production of vitamin D, which is essential for life.  And it makes sense to think about it this way: vitamin D is produced by the interaction of UV light from the sun with cholesterol in our skin.  In very dark skin, the large amount of melanin will absorb some of this solar radiation before it can penetrate deeper into the skin to produce vitamin D. This is also why black people have to be very careful when they go to very cold countries, far north or south, because they produce less vitamin D. In the past, people with dark skin who left Africa, for example, might have survived less in more northerly environments and would not have been selected by evolution.  But what very recent studies have shown is that this story was, at the very least, incomplete.  The vitamin D hypothesis persists, but today scientists know that genes associated with fair skin already existed within Africa itself long before the great migration of Homo sapiens to Europe or Asia.  In other words, white skin didn't originate in Europe; it originated in Africa, in the genes of the first humans.  So much so that within Africa itself there are still populations today with extremely different skin tones , with a greater variation than the difference between an African and a European.  Did you know that? That's because Africa is a giant continent, and the southern part doesn't receive as much sunlight as the north. While some East African peoples, such as the Murce tribe, have the darkest skin tones ever recorded, groups like the S in southern Africa can have much lighter tones.  Another incredible discovery is that white skin, like that of the Europeans we know today, is very recent in history.  A study from 2025, look how recent that is.  He analyzed the DNA from human bones found in Europe and Asia and saw that dark skin was still predominant up to 3,000 years ago .  She was like most people.  The lighter skin only started to become more apparent after that.  But why? How did the population with darker skin survive there without producing as much vitamin D?  What we have here, guys, are hypotheses, okay?  The first explanation is that the Homo sapiens who arrived in Europe were hunter-gatherers.  They did not grow their own food; they had no agriculture.  They ate a lot of meat and fatty fish, naturally rich sources of vitamin D. Even with their dark skin, they obtained the vitamin D their bodies needed through their diet and were not eliminated by evolution.  Therefore, the dark skin would have lasted a long time because of that.  It was only with the arrival in Europe of lighter-skinned populations from Turkey and Russia that it began to mix and be slowly selected because it was in fact more advantageous in an environment with low sun exposure.  These people from Turkey and Russia had light skin because they were already farming populations with a diet low in fish and vitamin D. In other words, the main hypothesis today is that the gene for white skin was selected in these populations and then spread to the whole world.  It would not have originated in Europe. The truth is that skin color is one of the most diverse traits that exist in human beings.  We know that humans originated as Black people on the African continent, but at least 26 genes have already been linked to melanin and identified as relevant in determining pigmentation.  And the number continues to grow.  Even on the African continent, diversity was already enormous 300,000 years ago.  Each of these gene combinations producing this diversity was randomly selected, chosen, or eliminated by evolution over hundreds of thousands of years in response to where the group lived, what it ate, how much sun it received, and who it interacted with.  In fact, this last part is worth mentioning.  In the early years of Homo sapiens, we didn't just mix with others of our own species.  We form couples with Neanderthals, Denisovans, and other species of humans that science is still discovering.  Skin color, which today generates so much racism and discrimination, was, at the beginning of our history, just a small detail compared to much greater differences.  And it's a good thing we're here to tell this story. If you enjoyed this unique video about human evolution, let me know in the comments what else you'd like to know about this topic. Let's color the world with knowledge and say hello to science.

In other words, among players who are already very good, more training doesn't make such a significant difference between those who will become the next Neymar, Messi, or Cristiano, and those who will simply become top-level professionals.  What differentiates the top players from other professional players lies in other factors.  Biological predisposition and the quality of training and feedback they receive do matter, but the developmental environment, competitive experiences throughout life, and health over the years, along with psychological maturity, make an even bigger difference.  Remove any of these factors from the equation and you no longer have a star player.  But does Neymar have those characteristics?  I'm going to tell you about a study that put Neymar in an MRI machine to understand how his brain works, and that tells us a lot about how neuroscience can tell if a person will be a star player or not.  And the results of this study might change your opinion about Neymar. In 2014, scientists placed Neymar, who was 22 years old at the time, inside an MRI machine. Along with him were three other professional athletes, two elite swimmers, and an amateur soccer player.  The task was simple: rotate your right ankle, and the machine would assess which regions of the brain were being activated and working most at that moment.  What did the scientists see?  Do you think Neymar's brain was more or less active when he moved his ankle compared to the other participants? Guys, Neymar's brain activation was the lowest of all participants, both in terms of the number of active areas and intensity, meaning the energy consumed by the neurons.  At first, this might seem bad—that it involves less brain activity, making movements that are important for playing ball—but it's actually the opposite.  The data showed that Neymar's brain was able to perform the same movement with far less mental effort than any other participant. Scientists have called this extremely efficient motor control, as if the gesture were already so automated that the brain no longer needs to exert any effort to make it happen.  If the brain doesn't need to expend much effort on controlling the motor function of the legs, it can allocate the rest of its brain capacity to other things.
